title: Anger When Your Child Is the One Bullying
dek: When the school calls and the words land like a stone in your chest, the anger that rises isn't simple. It's layered with shame, confusion, and a fierce protective instinct that suddenly has nowhere to go. This piece maps the terrain of that moment and what becomes possible when you stop running from the heat.
bodyMarkdown:
TL;DR. Learning your child is the one doing the bullying triggers a complex anger response that often goes underground as shame, denial, or defensive rationalization. This anger carries vital information about boundaries, modeling, and the nervous system patterns we pass down without realizing it. Working with this anger directly, rather than suppressing it, opens a path toward accountability, repair, and genuinely protective parenting.

She was folding laundry when her phone rang. The assistant principal's voice was careful, measured, using phrases like "concerning behavior" and "pattern we've noticed." Her daughter had been cornering a classmate at recess, whispering threats, rallying other kids to exclude her. The woman kept folding, creasing each shirt with precision, while something hot and jagged moved through her ribcage. She heard herself saying all the right things, thanking the school, promising to address it. But her hands were shaking, and she couldn't name what she felt. It wasn't until hours later, after her daughter was asleep, that she realized the tightness in her jaw was fury, and she had no idea where to put it. ## The Anger You Weren't Expecting When you discover your child is bullying someone, the anger that surfaces rarely announces itself clearly. It arrives sideways, dressed as defensiveness or rationalization. You might find yourself minimizing the behavior, explaining it away as "kids being kids," or immediately pivoting to blame the school, the other child, the circumstances. This defensive posture is actually anger in disguise, a nervous system response to the sudden threat to your identity as a good parent. In my own practice, I've watched parents move through a predictable sequence. First comes the shock, the disbelief. Then a hot flush of shame that someone else has seen what you missed. And underneath that, a white-hot anger that has multiple targets at once: your child, yourself, the situation, the messenger. The anger is complicated because it's entangled with love, with the fierce instinct to protect your child even when they're the one causing harm. Thomas Moore reminds us that anger is often the soul's response to a boundary violation, a sacred "no" that needs expression. When your child bullies, multiple boundaries have been crossed. Your child has violated another person's safety. But there's also a boundary violation in your own psyche: the gap between who you thought your child was and who they're revealing themselves to be in this moment. That dissonance creates its own kind of rage. The body knows before the mind catches up. You might notice your breath getting shallow, your shoulders climbing toward your ears, a clenching in your solar plexus. These somatic signals are your anger asking for attention, and they carry information you need. ## What This Anger Is Actually Protecting Anger, at its root, is a protective emotion. It arises to defend something we value. When your child is the bully, your anger is trying to protect multiple things simultaneously, and that's part of what makes it so disorienting. You're angry because you value kindness, and your child has acted cruelly. You're angry because you value your own competence as a parent, and this situation suggests you've missed something critical. You're angry at your child for putting themselves at risk, for damaging their reputation, for creating consequences they don't yet understand. And often, buried deeper, you're angry at yourself for whatever patterns or wounds you might have passed down without meaning to. Gabor Maté's work on trauma and development shows us that children who bully are often acting out unprocessed pain or modeling behavior they've absorbed from their environment. This doesn't excuse the behavior, but it does add another layer to your anger. If your child is hurting others because they're hurting, then your protective anger has to stretch wide enough to hold both realities: your child needs accountability and your child needs help. Over the years I've seen parents get stuck in a loop where they're so angry at their child that they can't access compassion, or so desperate to be compassionate that they bypass accountability entirely. The anger becomes either a weapon or a forbidden feeling. Neither approach serves anyone. The practice is to let the anger be present while you figure out what it's protecting and what it's asking you to do. Across the articles we've published on this site, one pattern shows up consistently: unprocessed anger in parents creates a chaotic emotional field that children absorb and often act out. If you've been suppressing your own anger for years, teaching your child through modeling that anger is dangerous or unacceptable, they may be expressing it in the only way they know how - through aggression toward someone with less power. ## The Shame Spiral and Why It Matters Shame is anger turned inward, and when you learn your child is bullying, shame often arrives faster than conscious anger. You feel exposed, judged, fundamentally flawed. And shame is one of the most corrosive emotional states there is because it shuts down curiosity, creativity, and the capacity for repair. When you're in shame, you can't think clearly about what your child needs. You're too busy defending against the internal narrative that you're a terrible parent, that you've failed, that everyone can see your inadequacy. Shame makes you reactive. You might lash out at your child in ways that mirror the very bullying behavior you're trying to address. Or you might collapse into helplessness, unable to set the firm boundaries the situation requires. The relationship between shame and anger is particularly important here. Research documented in the NIH overview of anger and the autonomic nervous system shows how quickly shame can trigger a fight-or-flight response, flooding your system with stress hormones that make measured, thoughtful parenting nearly impossible. One of the most useful things you can do in this moment is notice when shame is driving the bus. It usually sounds like absolutes: "I'm a terrible parent." "My child is fundamentally bad." "This is irreparable." When you hear those thoughts, you're in shame territory. The antidote isn't positive thinking or self-reassurance. It's bringing your attention back to your body, to the physical sensation of what you're feeling, and letting yourself be angry without the story that the anger means something catastrophic about your worth. ## What Your Body Knows About This Your body is holding information about this situation that your conscious mind hasn't processed yet. When you think about your child's behavior, where does the sensation live in your body? Some parents feel it as a tight band across their chest, a constriction that makes breathing difficult. Others feel heat in their face and neck, or a clenching in their jaw so intense they wake up with headaches. Pat Ogden's work in somatic psychology teaches us that the body stores patterns of response that bypass conscious thought. If you experienced bullying as a child, either as victim or perpetrator or witness, your body remembers. When your own child bullies, you're not just responding to the current situation. You're also responding to the unresolved charge from your own history. Try this: Sit quietly for a moment and bring to mind the fact that your child has hurt someone. Notice what happens in your body without trying to change it. Does your breath catch? Do your hands clench? Does your belly tighten? Just observe. This isn't about fixing anything yet. It's about gathering information. The anger you feel has a physical signature, and that signature is unique to you. Some people feel anger as heat, an expansive energy that wants to move outward. Others feel it as a cold, sharp contraction, a pulling away. There's no right way to feel anger. But knowing how your anger lives in your body gives you a starting point for working with it instead of being ambushed by it. When you're regulated enough to be present with your child, you'll notice your body softens slightly even while maintaining firmness. Your jaw unclenches. Your breathing deepens. You can hold eye contact without it feeling like a challenge. These somatic markers tell you that you're in a place where accountability and compassion can coexist. ## The Practice: Five Minutes With Your Anger Here's something you can do right now, or the next time the anger surges up and you need a way to be with it that doesn't involve acting it out or stuffing it down. Find a place where you won't be interrupted. Sit or stand in a way that feels stable. Set a timer for five minutes. Close your eyes if that feels okay, or soften your gaze toward the floor. Bring your attention to your breath without trying to change it. Just notice the inhale, the exhale. Now bring to mind the situation with your child. Let the anger be there. Say internally, "I'm angry." Notice where you feel it in your body. Put your hand on that place if it helps. The chest, the belly, the throat, wherever the sensation is strongest. Breathe into that place. Not to make the anger go away, but to give it space, to let it be as big as it is. If words want to come, let them come silently. "I'm so angry." "This is so hard." "I don't know what to do." You're not trying to solve anything in this moment. You're just being with the truth of what you feel. When the timer goes off, take one more deep breath and thank yourself for showing up for this. This practice won't fix the situation with your child, but it will shift your relationship to your own anger. And that shift is the foundation for everything else that needs to happen. ## Accountability Without Annihilation One of the hardest balancing acts in parenting is holding your child accountable without shaming them into a corner where change becomes impossible. Your anger, when it's conscious and directed, can actually help with this. It gives you the energy to set firm boundaries and follow through with consequences. But if the anger is unconscious or unprocessed, it leaks out as contempt, sarcasm, or punitive harshness that damages your child's sense of self without teaching them anything useful. Accountability means your child faces real consequences for their behavior. They apologize to the person they hurt, and the apology has to be genuine, not performed. They lose privileges. They have supervised interactions until trust is rebuilt. They might work with a counselor or participate in a restorative justice process at school. The specifics will depend on the severity and context of the bullying, but the through-line is this: your child learns that their actions have impact, and that impact matters. But accountability without connection becomes punishment for its own sake. Your child needs to know that you're angry about what they did, not about who they are. This distinction is everything. "You hurt someone, and that's not okay" is different from "You're a bad person." The first opens a door to change. The second slams it shut. In our experience, the parents who navigate this most skillfully are the ones who can access their anger, express it clearly, and then return to a baseline of connection. They don't pretend they're not angry. They don't minimize the behavior to preserve the relationship. They say, "I love you, and I'm furious about what you did, and we're going to figure this out together." This is also where your own work matters immensely. If you're carrying unprocessed anger from your own childhood, if you were bullied or shamed or punished harshly, that history will influence how you respond to your child now. You might find resources in [the fire toolkit](/fire-toolkit) that help you separate your past from your present so you can show up for your child with clarity. ## The Patterns You Didn't Mean to Teach Children learn how to handle anger by watching the adults around them. If you've modeled suppression, pretending everything is fine when it's not, your child learns that anger is dangerous and must be hidden. If you've modeled explosion, losing your temper in ways that feel out of control, your child learns that anger is a force that justifies harm. If you've modeled passive aggression, making cutting remarks or withdrawing affection when you're upset, your child learns that anger is something you inflict indirectly. Bullying is often a child's attempt to have power in a situation where they feel powerless. And powerlessness is frequently rooted in an environment where emotions, especially anger, aren't allowed to be expressed directly and safely. When a child can't say "I'm angry" and have that anger received without punishment or dismissal, the anger goes underground. It comes out sideways, often toward someone who can't fight back. This doesn't mean you caused your child to bully. Children are complex beings with their own temperaments, their own social dynamics, their own choices. But it does mean that examining your own relationship with anger is part of the healing process. How do you handle conflict? How do you express anger? What did you learn about anger from your own parents, and what are you unconsciously passing down? One mother I worked with realized that she'd spent years telling her son to "be nice" whenever he expressed frustration or anger, effectively training him that his anger was unacceptable. He learned to smile and comply on the surface, and then he started targeting a smaller, quieter kid at school. The bullying was his anger finding the only outlet it could. Her work wasn't just about consequences for her son. It was about learning to welcome his anger, to help him express it directly and safely, so it didn't have to come out as cruelty. If you're looking for structured ways to explore your own emotional patterns, you might find the tools in [the assessments hub](/assessments) helpful as a starting point for this kind of self-inquiry. ## When Your Anger Reveals What Needs to Change Sometimes the anger you feel when you learn your child is bullying is pointing toward something that needs to shift in your family system, not just in your child's behavior. Maybe there's too much stress, too little connection, too many hours of screen time and not enough face-to-face interaction. Maybe there's unresolved conflict between you and your partner that your child is absorbing and acting out. Maybe your child is overscheduled, exhausted, and using aggression as a way to feel some sense of control. Your anger can be a diagnostic tool if you're willing to let it show you what's true. It's uncomfortable to consider that the structure of your family life might be contributing to your child's behavior, but discomfort is often where growth begins. This isn't about blame. It's about responsibility, which is different. Responsibility means you have the ability to respond, to make changes, to create an environment where your child can learn a different way of being. For some families, this means slowing down. Creating more space for unstructured time, for boredom, for the kind of play that doesn't have a goal or a screen. For others, it means addressing the tension in the house that everyone feels but no one talks about. For others still, it means getting support, whether that's family therapy, parenting classes, or individual work for you or your child or both. One practical shift that supports nervous system regulation is attending to basic rhythms: sleep, nutrition, movement, and downtime. A child who's chronically under-rested and over-stimulated is more likely to act out aggressively. Something as simple as a consistent bedtime routine with [Gaia Herbs Sound Sleep](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B0036THNRA?tag=spankyspinola-20) can begin to restore a sense of safety and regulation that makes different behavior possible. ## Repair Is a Practice, Not a Destination After the initial crisis, after the conversations and consequences and apologies, there's the long work of repair. This is where many parents lose steam. The anger has subsided into a dull ache, the urgency has faded, and it's tempting to declare the problem solved and move on. But repair isn't a single event. It's an ongoing practice of rebuilding trust, monitoring patterns, and staying connected even when it's hard. Repair means checking in with your child regularly about how they're feeling, what's happening in their social world, whether they're struggling in ways they haven't told you about. It means staying in contact with teachers and school staff, not as surveillance but as collaboration. It means noticing when old patterns start to resurface and intervening early, before they escalate. It also means repairing your relationship with your own anger. If you've been at war with your anger, seeing it as a problem to be solved or a feeling to be avoided, this situation is an invitation to a different relationship. Anger, when it's met with curiosity and care, becomes a source of energy and clarity. It tells you what matters. It mobilizes you to protect what you love. It draws boundaries and insists on respect. There are practices that support this kind of repair work on a physiological level. Herbal support like [Banyan Botanicals Avipattikar Powder - 1/2 lb](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001FAYUVO?tag=spankyspinola-20) can help cool an overheated system, especially if you've been running hot with stress and frustration for weeks or months. Evening rituals with [Numi Chamomile Lemon Tea (18 ct)](https://www.amazon.com/dp/B001I4EBAW?tag=spankyspinola-20) can signal to your nervous system that it's time to downshift, to let go of the day's intensity. The community at [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com) offers additional perspectives on working with difficult emotions in the context of relationship and spiritual practice, if that's a lens that lands for you. ## Frequently Asked Questions ### How do I talk to my child about their bullying without making them defensive? Start by getting curious instead of accusatory. Ask open questions: "What was happening for you when you said that to her?" "What do you think she felt when that happened?" Listen more than you talk in the first conversation. Your child is more likely to open up if they don't feel like they're walking into an ambush. You can be clear that the behavior is unacceptable and still create space for your child to tell you their side. The goal is understanding first, consequences second. When children feel understood, even in the midst of accountability, they're more likely to take responsibility and make different choices. ### What if I'm angrier at the school for how they handled it than I am at my child? That's a common response, and it's worth examining. Sometimes the school does handle things poorly, and your anger is a reasonable response to their incompetence or overreach. But often, anger at the school is a deflection, a way to avoid the more painful anger at your child or yourself. Notice if your anger at the school is giving you permission to minimize what your child did. If you find yourself spending more energy fighting the school than addressing your child's behavior, that's a sign something's off. You can advocate for your child and hold them accountable at the same time. They're not mutually exclusive. ### How long will it take for my child to change this behavior? There's no fixed timeline, and anyone who promises you one is lying. Some children have a wake-up moment and genuinely shift their behavior quickly. Others need months or years of consistent support, boundaries, and intervention. The question isn't really about how long it will take. It's about whether you're willing to stay engaged for as long as it takes. Change happens in layers. Your child might stop the overt bullying but still struggle with empathy or impulse control. They might make progress and then regress under stress. Your job is to be steady, to keep showing up, to believe in their capacity to grow even when the evidence is thin. That kind of faith, combined with clear expectations, is what makes change possible. ## What Opens Next There's a moment in every fire when the logs shift, when what was burning hot and bright collapses into coals. The heat is still there, but it's different now. Deeper, steadier, capable of lasting through the night. Your anger about your child's bullying will shift like that if you let it. The initial blaze of shock and fury will settle into something more enduring: a commitment to truth, to accountability, to the slow work of helping your child become someone who doesn't hurt others to feel strong. You're not in this alone, even when it feels isolating. There are [more articles in this journal](/articles) that explore the territory of anger, parenting, and the places where they intersect. There are other parents navigating this same heartbreak, asking the same hard questions, doing the same unglamorous work of showing up day after day even when the path forward isn't clear. The anger you feel is part of your love for your child. It's the part that refuses to accept cruelty as inevitable, that insists on something better. Let it guide you, not as a weapon but as a compass, pointing toward the kind of parent and the kind of family you're becoming through this fire. ---
Written by **The Oracle Lover**, independent essayist on anger, somatics, and embodied spirituality. Last updated May 28, 2026. In our work on anger when your child is the one bullying, we keep returning to one quiet truth: the body has been telling you something for a long time, and it deserves a thoughtful listener. The Anger Practice is a companion journal to [theoraclelover.com](https://theoraclelover.com), and every piece here is written with you in mind.
